summary: This post provides a practical framework for managers to determine performance ratings fairly and thoughtfully. Fournier advocates for combining three approaches: goal-setting with tiered expectations (must-achieve, stretch, and moonshot goals), evaluating against consistent role-specific attributes, and applying personal judgment. She emphasizes that no perfect algorithm exists for rating people, and managers must reconcile objective data with their gut reactions to uncover hidden biases. The process requires starting early with clear expectations and being prepared to defend ratings during calibration exercises.
key_points: Goal-setting should use tiered categories (must-achieve, stretch, moonshot) to create clear benchmarks for different rating levels
Evaluating people against consistent attributes helps identify strengths and weaknesses, but people don't fit neatly into boxes
Senior managers should be judged partly on their ability to anticipate and course-correct when teams face challenges
Comparing data-driven assessments against gut reactions reveals both rating biases and unstated role requirements
The rating process starts long before evaluation time, with clear goal-setting and role alignment
money_quotes: As with all things in the world of people management, there is no perfect algorithm you can apply to ensure total fairness and accuracy.
Only you can tell the difference between a manager who was blindsided by events and a manager who just failed to plan well, or who couldn't course correct their team effectively.
The most interesting and useful part of this exercise is comparing what you get from this data against your gut reaction to the rating you think someone should get.
You do not want to set yourself up to deny good ratings to people doing good work when they don't fit perfectly in a box.
If you are open-minded, the data will show you that you're off both high and low in different cases.

Sometimes, it will force you to acknowledge your own bias: why do I always want to give this kind of person a lower rating even though objectively their work looks as good as this other kind of person?
key_insight: Fair performance ratings require combining structured frameworks with honest self-examination of your own judgment and biases.
